Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or until something was due soon. With five online courses and my extracurricular stuff going on, I never really nailed down a consistent schedule. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often just dive into assignments without much planning. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was coming up. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving, not any kind of regular schedule. With my limited resources and everything else going on, I couldn't really commit to studying at the same time every week. I'd have bursts of activity followed by stretches where I barely touched anything academic. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I operated.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd go in just to watch videos when I was bored or had some downtime. I also logged in to check discussion posts since I actually enjoyed those interactions with classmates. But it wasn't like a daily habit or anything systematic.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking in regularly, especially when discussions were active and people were responding to my posts. That social element made it feel less like a chore. But honestly, that routine never stuck for long because life would get in the way or I'd lose interest. The mindfulness stuff I was doing helped me be more present when I did engage though.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me because I could watch them at my own pace and rewind when I needed to. I watched almost five hours worth, which is a lot for me. The discussion boards were also valuable because talking things through with peers helped concepts click better than just reading. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything interactive worked better than static content.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. They just didn't hold my attention, and I don't have great resources like a printer or a quiet space to really dig into dense text. I'd skim them at best or just skip to the parts that seemed relevant to assignments. The more passive stuff just didn't fit how I learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to something I'd actually need to do or know. If it was going to help me with an assignment or if it involved actually doing something rather than just absorbing information, I was in. The discussions felt worthwhile because I was actively participating, not just consuming. Anything that felt like busywork got pushed aside.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. If something wasn't required, I'd pretty much skip it unless it genuinely interested me. With everything else on my plate, I had to prioritize, and optional stuff rarely made the cut. Required activities at least had the pressure of grades behind them, which honestly was the main thing that got me to do them.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded videos or materials, I'd often study them offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd take notes on paper and work through problems by hand since that kinesthetic approach works better for me anyway. I also used some external YouTube videos and resources that explained things in different ways. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or discussing concepts with friends in person, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd download a lecture once and then spend hours with it offline. Sometimes I'd be at my extracurricular activities and we'd end up talking about course-related stuff, which was learning too. The LMS only saw a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd download videos or PDFs once and then work with them offline for days. My internet situation isn't great, so it made more sense to grab everything I needed in one session and then study without being connected. The LMS would show one quick login, but I'd actually spend hours with that material later.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when stress piled up from multiple courses and life stuff. My resilience isn't the greatest, so when things got overwhelming, I'd kind of check out for a while. Mid-semester was particularly rough when everything seemed due at once. The mindfulness practice helped me get through some of those moments, but I still struggled to bounce back quickly.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or stressed, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and deal with coursework. My other responsibilities often took priority because they felt more immediate. Confusion about course material would make me avoid it rather than dig in, which I know isn't great. The workload from five courses was honestly too much sometimes.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what anyone was saying. Sometimes I'd log in, click around a bit, and then realize I hadn't actually engaged with anything meaningful. Being present is harder than it sounds.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially the stuff that felt overwhelming or confusing. Even knowing something was important, I'd put it off because I didn't have the mental energy to tackle it. My low resilience meant that when something felt hard, my instinct was to avoid it rather than push through. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, and sometimes later never came.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd barely touch the LMS until something was due soon, then I'd cram everything into a short window. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was the only thing that consistently got me to act. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and do other things.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But if feedback was negative or I felt like I'd failed at something, it made me want to avoid that area. I'm not great at bouncing back from setbacks, so critical feedback sometimes pushed me away rather than pulling me in. Positive reinforcement worked way better for me.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having five online courses at once made it hard to keep track of everything. When things were clearly laid out with obvious deadlines, I could manage. But when the structure was confusing or requirements weren't clear, I'd disengage pretty quickly. I needed things to be straightforward because I didn't have the bandwidth for complexity.
Q19: The discussion components made engagement easier because they felt social and active. Video content was also accessible for me. What made it harder was when there was too much reading or when instructions were vague. Having limited resources meant I couldn't always access things the way they were designed to be accessed, which created barriers.
Q20: I think sometimes people are genuinely trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. They might be watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand the material, but when it comes to actually completing assignments, something breaks down. Could be confusion, could be life stuff, could be anxiety about submitting imperfect work. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I didn't have super high LMS activity, but I passed and did okay on assignments. A lot of my studying happened offline after I downloaded materials. I also learned from peers outside the system. So yeah, low visible activity doesn't necessarily mean someone isn't learning or engaging with the content.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, maybe how long you spent on certain pages. But they miss all the offline work, the thinking you do away from the screen, the conversations with classmates. They can't measure understanding or genuine engagement. They see the surface but not the depth.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental energy into understanding it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. It's about being present and actively trying to make sense of things. Some of my most engaged moments happened when I wasn't even near a computer, just thinking through problems or discussing ideas.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned from offline study or peer conversations. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness work I did to manage stress and stay focused. It wouldn't show that despite my scattered attendance, I was genuinely happy and interested in the content when I did engage. The human stuff, the real learning, happens in places data can't reach.
